annotations.label: Euclidean algorithm

anchor.snippet: is an efficient method for computing the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers

annotations.note: Mathlib's gcd (and Nat.gcd) is defined by the recursive Euclidean algorithm via well-founded recursion on remainders.

annotations.label: Subtraction principle for GCD

anchor.snippet: the greatest common divisor of two numbers does not change if the larger number is replaced by its difference with the smaller number

annotations.note: Nat.gcd_sub_self_left/right state the gcd is unchanged when one argument is replaced by its difference with the other.

annotations.label: GCD of 252 and 105

anchor.snippet: 21 is the GCD of 252 and 105

annotations.note: A specific numeric example, decidable by computation but not a named Mathlib result.

annotations.label: Bézout's identity (stated)

anchor.snippet: The fact that the GCD can always be expressed in this way is known as Bézout's identity

annotations.note: Int.gcd_eq_gcd_ab expresses gcd x y = x * gcdA x y + y * gcdB x y.

annotations.label: Greatest common divisor

anchor.snippet: The greatest common divisor g is the largest natural number that divides both a and b without leaving a remainder

annotations.note: Nat.gcd with gcd_dvd_left/right and dvd_gcd captures the greatest common divisor.

annotations.label: Coprime numbers

anchor.snippet: then a and b are said to be coprime (or relatively prime)

annotations.note: Nat.Coprime a b is defined as gcd a b = 1; the ring-level analogue is IsCoprime.

annotations.label: 6 and 35 are coprime

anchor.snippet: 6 and 35 are coprime

annotations.note: Specific numeric example, decidable but not a named result.

annotations.label: GCD divisible by every common divisor

anchor.snippet: The greatest common divisor g of a and b is the unique (positive) common divisor of a and b that is divisible by any other common divisor

annotations.note: Nat.dvd_gcd states any common divisor of a and b divides gcd a b (universal property).

annotations.label: Geometric visualization of GCD

anchor.snippet: The greatest common divisor can be visualized as follows

annotations.note: An informal geometric picture, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: GCD via shared prime factors

anchor.snippet: is the product of the prime factors shared by the two numbers

annotations.note: Nat.factorization_gcd gives (gcd a b).factorization = a.factorization ⊓ b.factorization, i.e. the min of shared prime exponents.

annotations.label: GCD of 1386 and 3213

anchor.snippet: the GCD of 1386 and 3213 equals 63

annotations.note: Specific numeric example, not a named result.

annotations.label: GCD as smallest linear combination

anchor.snippet: is also their smallest positive integral linear combination

annotations.note: Int.gcd_least_linear proves gcd a b is the least positive value in {a*x+b*y}.

annotations.label: GCD of three or more numbers

anchor.snippet: The GCD of three or more numbers equals the product of the prime factors common to all the numbers

annotations.note: Finset.gcd generalizes gcd to finitely many elements, but the prime-factor characterization for multiple numbers is not a named lemma.

annotations.label: Algorithm as remainder sequence

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean algorithm can be thought of as constructing a sequence of non-negative integers

annotations.note: Nat.gcd_rec (gcd m n = gcd (n % m) m) encodes the remainder recursion underlying the algorithm.

annotations.label: Algorithm terminates

anchor.snippet: the algorithm will always terminate at the n th step

annotations.note: Termination is guaranteed by the well-founded recursion defining Nat.gcd, but there is no standalone termination theorem.

annotations.label: GCD of 1071 and 462 (procedure)

anchor.snippet: suppose the GCD of 1071 and 462 is requested

annotations.note: Specific numeric worked example, not a named result.

annotations.label: Dropping the ordering requirement

anchor.snippet: We can generalize slightly by dropping any ordering requirement on the initial two values

annotations.note: Nat.gcd_comm shows gcd is symmetric, so the inputs need not be ordered.

annotations.label: Termination of the algorithm

anchor.snippet: This ensures that the algorithm terminates

annotations.note: Strictly decreasing remainders correspond to the well-founded recursion in Nat.gcd's definition, but no explicit termination lemma is stated.

annotations.label: Last nonzero remainder is the GCD

anchor.snippet: follows from the following properties of

annotations.note: The recursion Nat.gcd_rec computes the last nonzero remainder, proven equal to the gcd via gcd_dvd_left/right and dvd_gcd.

annotations.label: Property 1: common divisors of a pair

anchor.snippet: In fact, the common divisors of

annotations.note: Together with gcd_dvd_left/right, Nat.dvd_gcd shows the common divisors of a,b are exactly the divisors of gcd a b.

annotations.label: Property 2: invariance of common divisors

anchor.snippet: the sets of common divisors of the pairs

annotations.note: Nat.gcd_rec gives gcd a b = gcd b (a % b), reflecting that the pair (a,b) and (b, a mod b) share common divisors.

annotations.label: Worked example gcd(1071, 462)

anchor.snippet: the Euclidean algorithm can be used to find the greatest common divisor of a = 1071 and b = 462

annotations.note: Specific numeric worked example, not a named result.

annotations.label: Tiling visualization

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean algorithm can be visualized in terms of the tiling analogy

annotations.note: An informal visualization, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Euclidean division step

anchor.snippet: the Euclidean algorithm computes a quotient q k and remainder r k

annotations.note: div_add_mod states b*(a/b) + a%b = a, the quotient-remainder relation of one division step (Nat.div_add_mod for naturals).

annotations.label: Existence and uniqueness of quotient/remainder

anchor.snippet: ensures that such a quotient and remainder always exist and are unique

annotations.note: Existence is Int.ediv_add_emod / Nat.div_add_mod and uniqueness is Int.ediv_emod_unique (Nat.div_mod_unique).

annotations.label: Least absolute remainder variant

anchor.snippet: the quotient at each step is increased by one if the resulting negative remainder is smaller in magnitude

annotations.note: The least-absolute-remainder (balanced) variant of the Euclidean algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: Remainder halving bound

anchor.snippet: then one gets a variant of Euclidean algorithm such that

annotations.note: The remainder-halving bound for the balanced variant is not formalized.

annotations.label: Kronecker: fewest steps

anchor.snippet: this version requires the fewest steps of any version of Euclid's algorithm

annotations.note: Kronecker's optimality of the least-absolute-remainder version is not formalized.

annotations.label: Minimal steps iff golden-ratio quotient

anchor.snippet: the number of steps is minimal if and only if q k is chosen in order that

annotations.note: The golden-ratio characterization of minimal step count is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bézout's identity

anchor.snippet: the greatest common divisor g of two integers a and b can be represented as a linear sum of the original two numbers

annotations.note: Int.gcd_eq_gcd_ab gives gcd a b = a*s + b*t with s,t = gcdA, gcdB.

annotations.label: Computing s and t by back-substitution

anchor.snippet: The integers s and t can be calculated from the quotients

annotations.note: Nat.xgcd (via xgcdAux) computes the Bézout coefficients from the algorithm's quotients.

annotations.label: Generalization to Euclidean domains

anchor.snippet: can be generalized to the context of Euclidean domains

annotations.note: EuclideanDomain.gcd_eq_gcd_ab states Bézout's identity for any Euclidean domain.

annotations.label: GCD as ideal generator

anchor.snippet: Bézout's identity provides yet another definition of the greatest common divisor

annotations.note: span_gcd proves Ideal.span {gcd x y} = Ideal.span {x, y}, so gcd generates the ideal (x,y).

annotations.label: Principal ideal and PID

anchor.snippet: the modern abstract algebraic concepts of a principal ideal

annotations.note: Submodule.IsPrincipal and IsPrincipalIdealRing formalize principal ideals and PIDs.

annotations.label: Measuring cups

anchor.snippet: consider two measuring cups of volume a and b

annotations.note: An illustrative physical example, not a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Extended Euclidean algorithm

anchor.snippet: can be computed efficiently using the extended Euclidean algorithm

annotations.note: Nat.xgcd (and EuclideanDomain.xgcd) implement the extended Euclidean algorithm.

annotations.label: Validity by induction

anchor.snippet: The validity of this approach can be shown by induction

annotations.note: Nat.gcd_eq_gcd_ab proves correctness of the extended algorithm's output coefficients.

annotations.label: Matrix method

anchor.snippet: can also be found using an equivalent matrix method

annotations.note: The 2×2 matrix formulation of the algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: Determinant of M

anchor.snippet: it equals the product of the determinants of the quotient matrices

annotations.note: The determinant identity for the quotient-matrix product is not formalized.

annotations.label: Euclid's lemma

anchor.snippet: if a prime number divides L , then it must divide at least one factor of L

annotations.note: Nat.Prime.dvd_mul: p prime ∧ p ∣ m*n ↔ p ∣ m ∨ p ∣ n (general Prime.dvd_or_dvd also holds).

annotations.label: Coprimality preserved by products

anchor.snippet: then w is also coprime to their product

annotations.note: Nat.Coprime.mul_right (and IsCoprime.mul_right) prove coprimality is preserved under products.

annotations.label: Unique factorization into primes

anchor.snippet: every number has a unique factorization into prime numbers

annotations.note: Existence is factorization_prod_pow_eq_self and uniqueness is Nat.factorization_inj (the general UniqueFactorizationMonoid framework also applies).

annotations.label: Diophantine equation

anchor.snippet: Diophantine equations are equations in which the solutions are restricted to integers

annotations.note: There is no general 'Diophantine equation' definition (Mathlib formalizes specific equations and Diophantine sets, not this concept).

annotations.label: Solvability condition

anchor.snippet: c must also be divisible by g , or the equation has no solutions

annotations.note: Int.gcd_dvd_iff: gcd a b ∣ n ↔ ∃ x y, n = a*x + b*y, i.e. a*x+b*y=c solvable iff gcd divides c.

annotations.label: Number of solutions

anchor.snippet: a linear Diophantine equation has no solutions, or an infinite number of solutions

annotations.note: The parametrization/count of solutions to a linear Diophantine equation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Finite field

anchor.section: Multiplicative inverses and the RSA algorithm
anchor.snippet: A finite field is a set of numbers with four generalized operations

annotations.note: Finite fields are formalized as Field instances (e.g. ZMod p for prime p) with finiteness via Fintype.

annotations.label: Field of 13 numbers

anchor.section: Multiplicative inverses and the RSA algorithm
anchor.snippet: An example of a finite field is the set of 13 numbers

annotations.note: ZMod 13 is a field via ZMod.instField with the prime-fact instance for 13.

annotations.label: Modular multiplicative inverse

anchor.section: Multiplicative inverses and the RSA algorithm
anchor.snippet: every nonzero element a has a unique modular multiplicative inverse

annotations.note: ZMod.unitOfCoprime produces the modular inverse of an element coprime to the modulus.

annotations.label: Chinese remainder theorem

anchor.snippet: the Chinese remainder theorem , which describes a novel method to represent an integer

annotations.note: ZMod.chineseRemainder gives ZMod (m*n) ≃+* ZMod m × ZMod n for coprime m,n (general Ideal.quotientInfRingEquivPiQuotient also exists).

annotations.label: Stern–Brocot tree

anchor.snippet: into an infinite binary search tree , called the Stern–Brocot tree

annotations.note: The Stern–Brocot tree is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Each rational appears once

anchor.snippet: each positive rational number appears exactly once in this tree

annotations.note: Not formalized; the Stern–Brocot tree is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Locating 3/4 in the tree

anchor.snippet: 3/4 can be found by starting at the root

annotations.note: Not formalized; specific example in an unformalized structure.

annotations.label: Calkin–Wilf tree

anchor.snippet: another binary tree on the rational numbers called the Calkin–Wilf tree

annotations.note: The Calkin–Wilf tree is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Relationship to continued fractions

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean algorithm has a close relationship with continued fractions

annotations.note: GenContFract.of constructs the continued-fraction expansion via the same floor/remainder steps, but the explicit gcd↔continued-fraction correspondence is not a named theorem.

annotations.label: Continued fraction of 1071/462

anchor.snippet: the quotients q k were 2, 3 and 7, respectively

annotations.note: Specific numeric example, not a named result.

annotations.label: GCD in factorization algorithms

anchor.snippet: Calculating a greatest common divisor is an essential step in several integer factorization algorithms

annotations.note: Integer factorization algorithms (e.g. Pollard's rho) using gcd are not formalized.

annotations.label: Reynaud bound

anchor.snippet: the number of division steps on input ( u , v ) is bounded by v

annotations.note: Step-count bounds for the algorithm are not formalized.

annotations.label: Finck bound

anchor.snippet: the number of division steps is at most 2 log 2 v + 1

annotations.note: Finck's logarithmic step bound is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lamé's theorem

anchor.snippet: the number of steps required for completion is never more than five times the number h of base-10 digits of the smaller number b

annotations.note: Lamé's theorem on the step count is not formalized.

annotations.label: Quadratic time for large numbers

anchor.snippet: showing that it is also O ( h 2 )

annotations.note: Bit-complexity analysis of the algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: Step count invariant under scaling

anchor.snippet: the number of steps remains the same if a and b are multiplied by a common factor w

annotations.note: Nat.gcd_mul_left formalizes gcd(wa,wb)=w·gcd(a,b), but step-count invariance under scaling is not formalized.

annotations.label: Recursive step-count equation

anchor.snippet: The recursive nature of the Euclidean algorithm gives another equation

annotations.note: A step-count function with a recurrence is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fibonacci worst case

anchor.snippet: the smallest values of a and b for which this is true are the Fibonacci numbers

annotations.note: The worst-case (Fibonacci) input analysis of the algorithm is not formalized, though Nat.fib_gcd relates fib and gcd.

annotations.label: Five times the digit count

anchor.snippet: the number of steps in Euclid's algorithm can never be more than five times the number of its digits

annotations.note: This (Lamé-style) digit-count bound is not formalized.

annotations.label: First average T(a)

anchor.snippet: The first definition is the average time T ( a ) required to calculate the GCD

annotations.note: Average-case running-time definitions are not formalized.

annotations.label: Tau average over coprimes

anchor.snippet: a second average τ ( a ) is taken over all numbers coprime with a

annotations.note: This average over coprime inputs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Porter's constant

anchor.snippet: The constant C in this formula is called Porter's constant

annotations.note: Porter's constant is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Third average Y(n)

anchor.snippet: A third average Y ( n ) is defined as the mean number of steps

annotations.note: This mean-step-count average is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cost of dividing h-bit numbers

anchor.snippet: The computational expense of dividing h -bit numbers scales as O ( h ( ℓ + 1))

annotations.note: Per-step bit-cost analysis is not formalized.

annotations.label: Probability of a given quotient

anchor.snippet: The probability of a given quotient q is approximately ln | u /( u − 1) |

annotations.note: The quotient-distribution (Gauss–Kuzmin-type) estimate is not formalized.

annotations.label: Quadratic overall growth

anchor.snippet: the Euclid's algorithm grows quadratically ( h 2 ) with the average number of digits

annotations.note: Overall quadratic complexity analysis is not formalized.

annotations.label: Enumerating common divisors is inefficient

anchor.snippet: is to calculate all their common divisors

annotations.note: An efficiency remark, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Binary GCD algorithm

anchor.snippet: The binary GCD algorithm is an efficient alternative

annotations.note: The binary GCD algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quasilinear GCD algorithms

anchor.snippet: leads to quasilinear integer GCD algorithms

annotations.note: Quasilinear GCD algorithms are not formalized.

annotations.label: Generalization of the algorithm

anchor.snippet: it may be generalized to the real numbers, and to other mathematical objects

annotations.note: The EuclideanDomain class generalizes the algorithm to abstract rings with division and remainder.

annotations.label: Commensurable lengths

anchor.snippet: a and b are called commensurable lengths, otherwise they are incommensurable lengths

annotations.note: The notion of commensurable/incommensurable lengths is not formalized.

annotations.label: Termination iff ratio rational

anchor.snippet: If it does, the fraction a / b is a rational number

annotations.note: GenContFract.terminates_iff_rat: the continued-fraction (Euclidean) expansion of v terminates iff v is rational.

annotations.label: Convergents are best approximations

anchor.snippet: The convergent m k / n k is the best rational number approximation to a / b

annotations.note: Legendre's theorem (Real.exists_rat_eq_convergent: a sufficiently good rational approximation must be a convergent) is formalized, but the full best-approximation optimality is not stated as such.

annotations.label: GCD of polynomials

anchor.snippet: The greatest common divisor polynomial g ( x ) of two polynomials

annotations.note: Polynomials over a field form a EuclideanDomain, so EuclideanDomain.gcd gives the polynomial GCD.

annotations.label: Last nonzero remainder is polynomial GCD

anchor.snippet: The last nonzero remainder is the greatest common divisor of the original two polynomials

annotations.note: EuclideanDomain.gcd_dvd_left/right and dvd_gcd prove the algorithm's last nonzero remainder is the gcd, applying to polynomials over a field.

annotations.label: GCD of two quartic polynomials

anchor.snippet: consider the following two quartic polynomials

annotations.note: A specific worked polynomial example, not a named result.

annotations.label: Gaussian integers

anchor.snippet: The Gaussian integers are complex numbers of the form

annotations.note: GaussianInt := Zsqrtd (-1), notation ℤ[i], formalizes the Gaussian integers.

annotations.label: Unique factorization of Gaussian integers

anchor.snippet: Gaussian integers can be shown to be uniquely factorizable

annotations.note: ℤ[i] is a EuclideanDomain, hence a PID, hence a UFD via PrincipalIdealRing.to_uniqueFactorizationMonoid.

annotations.label: Norm function on Gaussian integers

anchor.snippet: a norm function f ( u + vi ) = u 2 + v 2 is defined

annotations.note: Zsqrtd.norm gives re² - d·im²; for d=-1 (Gaussian integers) this is u²+v².

annotations.label: Euclidean domain

anchor.snippet: is called a Euclidean domain if it forms a commutative ring R

annotations.note: The EuclideanDomain class formalizes commutative rings with quotient/remainder and the division identity.

annotations.label: Euclidean function

anchor.snippet: The generalized Euclidean algorithm requires a Euclidean function

annotations.note: Mathlib's EuclideanDomain uses a well-founded relation r in place of an explicit ℕ-valued Euclidean function.

annotations.label: Unique factorization in Euclidean domains

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of arithmetic applies to any Euclidean domain

annotations.note: Via EuclideanDomain → PID → UFD, unique factorization holds in any Euclidean domain.

annotations.label: Euclidean domain is a UFD

anchor.snippet: Any Euclidean domain is a unique factorization domain (UFD)

annotations.note: EuclideanDomain.to_principal_ideal_domain then PrincipalIdealRing.to_uniqueFactorizationMonoid give that any Euclidean domain is a UFD.

annotations.label: Euclidean domain is a PID

anchor.snippet: A Euclidean domain is always a principal ideal domain (PID)

annotations.note: EuclideanDomain.to_principal_ideal_domain is an instance making any Euclidean domain an IsPrincipalIdealRing.

annotations.label: Quadratic integers

anchor.snippet: Quadratic integers are generalizations of the Gaussian integers

annotations.note: Zsqrtd d formalizes ℤ[√d]; the full ring of quadratic integers for d ≡ 1 mod 4 is a slightly larger ring.

annotations.label: Norm-Euclidean quadratic rings

anchor.snippet: The norm-Euclidean rings of quadratic integers are exactly those where D is one of the values

annotations.note: The classification of norm-Euclidean quadratic integer rings is not formalized.

annotations.label: Weinberger's theorem

anchor.snippet: a quadratic integer ring with D > 0 is Euclidean if, and only if, it is a principal ideal domain

annotations.note: Weinberger's theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Common right divisor

anchor.snippet: They have a common right divisor δ if

annotations.note: Right/left divisors and noncommutative gcd are not formalized.

annotations.label: Two versions of the algorithm

anchor.snippet: there are two versions of the Euclidean algorithm, one for right divisors and one for left divisors

annotations.note: Noncommutative left/right versions of the algorithm are not formalized.

annotations.label: Bézout's identity (noncommutative)

anchor.snippet: the right gcd( α , β ) can be expressed as a linear combination of α and β

annotations.note: Bézout's identity for noncommutative rings is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lagrange's four-square theorem

anchor.snippet: Lagrange's four-square theorem , that every positive integer can be represented as a sum of four squares

annotations.note: Nat.sum_four_squares proves every natural is a sum of four squares.
